content.problemStatement:
Chef wants to play a game of linear chess on a one-dimensional board ― an infinite row of squares numbered by positive integers. In this game, he has a pawn, which is initially at a square KKK. There are also NNN other people (numbered 111 through NNN); Chef can choose one of them to play against. For each valid iii, the iii-th player would play in the following way:


Take a pawn and place it on a square PiP_iPi​.
Repeat the following move any number of times: move the pawn from its current square PiP_iPi​ squares forward, i.e. from a square sss, this player's pawn is moved to the square s+Pis+P_is+Pi​.
If this player moves their pawn to the square with Chef's pawn, then Chef's pawn is captured and he loses the game.
Unfortunately, Chef cannot move his pawn during the game, making him an easy target for other players. Given the starting positions of all N+1N+1N+1 players, find a player who can capture Chef's pawn in the smallest number of moves or determine that no player can capture his pawn.
constraints: K,P1,P2,…,PNK, P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NK,P1​,P2​,…,PN​ are pairwise distinct
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the starting square of one player that can beat Chef in the smallest number of turns, or −1-1−1 if no player can beat him.

If there are multiple solutions, you may find any one.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The player who starts at the position 222 can move to square 444 and then to square 666. The player who starts at the position 333 can move to square 666. The player at position 222 can capture Chef's pawn in 222 turns, whereas the player at position 333 can capture Chef's pawn in 111 turn. Therefore, the answer is 333.
Example case 2: No player can capture Chef's pawn.
